title: Perspectives on the role of the family
summary: 9699 — functionalist, Marxist, feminist, and New Right views of the family.
learningObjectives: Outline key functions of the family in society.
Evaluate feminist critiques of the family.
Assess New Right and postmodern views.

simpleExplanation.title: What is the family for?
simpleExplanation.summary: Theorists disagree whether the family stabilises society, reproduces inequality, or oppresses women — or whether diverse forms have replaced the 'traditional' unit.

flashcards.back: Primary socialisation + stabilisation of adult personality.
Reproduces labour force; absorbs anger; inheritance of property.
Family is primary site of patriarchal oppression (Dworkin, Greer).
Traditional nuclear family best — welfare undermines self-reliance (Murray).

sections.content: **Family perspectives** (3.1) opens Paper 2. Compare **at least two theories** in every essay. Use **Murdock/Parsons vs Marxist/feminist** as standard contrast.

sections.question: Assess functionalist views of the role of the family in society. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Functionalist case:** Murdock — **universal functions**; Parsons — **fit thesis** (family matches industrial society); **warm bath** theory — relieves workplace stress; **primary socialisation** of children.

**Criticisms:** **Marxist** — family serves **capitalism** (Zaretsky — private haven masks exploitation); **feminist** — Parsons ignores **gender division** and domestic violence; **historical** — nuclear family not universal (extended, matrifocal); **diversity** — same-sex, lone-parent families function well.

**Evaluation:** Functionalism identifies **important functions** but **rose-tinted** — assumes harmony, neglects conflict and power.

**Conclusion:** **Partially useful** as starting point but **outdated** for contemporary diverse families.
